_comment: Research portfolio: the small set of LIVE ideas, not a brainstorm dump. Prune aggressively -- an idea leaves via `validated` or `dropped`, and its conclusion lives in out/hypothesis*.tsv. `causal_distance` is measured from the CURRENT lineage (decoder/attention tweaks on RayDPT): near = same family, mid = different component of the same pipeline, far = different abstraction level or transferred from another field. Statuses: backlog | probing | candidate | verifying | validated | dropped | inconclusive.

ideas.origin: physical property of the sensing modality (measured, not searched)
ideas.target_bottleneck: time-of-flight quantisation in the input representation
ideas.general_hypothesis: Depth from echoes is fundamentally a time-of-flight measurement: a surface at distance d returns its echo at t = 2d/c. If the input representation cannot resolve t finely, no decoder -- however expressive -- can resolve d. The current pipeline may be starved of temporal information long before the model sees it.
ideas.detailed_hypothesis: CORRECTED (before any GPU was spent): the STFT's temporal RESOLUTION is set by the analysis WINDOW, not by the hop. The window support smears an echo over c*win/(2*sr) of one-way depth; the hop only sets how densely that smeared function is sampled. At the default win=400 (8.33ms) the smear is 1.417 m -- essentially equal to the achieved RMSE of 1.3207 m, which is why the window is the suspect. Shrinking the hop alone (160->40) leaves the smear at 1.417 m and merely oversamples. To sharpen time-of-flight one must shorten the WINDOW (win 400 -> 64 gives a 0.227 m smear), paying frequency resolution for it.
ideas.original_detailed_hypothesis_WRONG: Earlier version claimed hop=160 'quantises depth at 0.567m' and that shrinking the hop adds information. The hop quantum is real but is NOT the resolution limit; the window is. Kept here so the correction is auditable.
ideas.expected_metric_effect: RMSE falls (range error), NOT d1. Per D3 the interaural cues already buy d1 (azimuth); if a temporal change buys d1 instead of RMSE, it is not acting through time-of-flight.
ideas.main_falsification_condition: Neither arm improves RMSE -> temporal resolution is not the binding constraint, DROP. If BOTH arms improve equally, the gain is extra sampling density / capacity, not resolution, and the mechanism story is wrong even if the score moves.
ideas.minimal_falsifiable_test: Two arms on run_base.py (cheapest parent), control = E2 (win 400, hop 160, smear 1.417m). A: win 400, hop 40 -> T=71, smear UNCHANGED 1.417m (density only). B: win 64, hop 16 -> T=177, smear 0.227m (true resolution). The design DISSOCIATES sampling density from resolution: only B should improve RMSE if I1 is right.
ideas.prior_weakened_by: A training-free nearest-neighbour retrieval probe (400 train / 120 val) found hop=40 no better than hop=160 (composite 3.048 vs 3.016). But that instrument is INVALID -- its NN retrieval (rmse 2.20) is worse than the trivial mean-training-depth predictor (rmse 1.64), so it cannot detect anything. It is recorded as a failed instrument, not as evidence against I1. Separately, a quantisation argument cuts against the ORIGINAL framing: a 0.567m quantum implies ~0.16m RMSE if quantisation were binding, while the achieved RMSE is 1.32m. That arithmetic is what exposed the window/hop confusion.
ideas.budget_risk: low-medium. Model FLOPs/VRAM/in_ch unchanged (feature is resized to (256,512) regardless). But win=64,hop=16 yields T=177 frames -> more STFT work per sample, so dataloading is slower and fewer epochs fit the wall-clock budget. Report epochs_ran; per D2 epoch count is a confound.
ideas.evidence_required: Arm B (win 64) must improve RMSE while arm A (hop only) does not. A d1-only gain does NOT count (per D3, d1 is the cue-driven metric).
ideas.unblocked_by: The STFT analysis window is now editable research logic (--stft-nfft/--stft-hop/--stft-win) read from cfg.dataset. Defaults verified bit-identical; changing them leaves cut/depth/in_ch/shape untouched.
ideas.strengthened_by: (a) I2's oracle diagnostic: the decoder's 64x128 output resolution imposes a ceiling of composite 0.2399 while the model sits at 1.8567 -- the bottleneck is NOT downstream of the decoder, so it is upstream: information. (b) The S0 2x2 dissociation: interaural cues buy d1 (angle) and log1p buys rmse (range), yet the best rmse is still 1.3088 m -- and the analysis window smears an echo over 1.417 m of one-way depth, essentially the same size. Range error and window smear coincide.
ideas.next_action: RUNNING in the queue after E4: arm A = batvision_5ch_win400_hop40, arm B = batvision_5ch_win64_hop16. Control = E2.

ideas.origin: measured property of the planar target (grad-energy, 24 samples / 6 scenes)
ideas.target_bottleneck: (claimed) output resolution: 4x bilinear upsample smears the target's sharp steps
ideas.general_hypothesis: The planar target is piecewise-constant, so a decoder that cannot represent a step cannot fit it, and d1 punishes blurred edges. RayDPT decodes at 64x128 and upsamples 4x; add a 128x256 scale to halve that.
ideas.why_dropped: REFUTED by an oracle diagnostic that cost zero GPU time. Downsample the GROUND TRUTH to each decode resolution and bilinearly upsample it back: that is what a PERFECT predictor at that resolution would score. At RayDPT's 64x128 the oracle scores d1=0.9757, RMSE=0.2873m, composite=0.2399. The best ACHIEVED is d1=0.5938, RMSE=1.3207m, composite=1.8646. The model is nowhere near the ceiling its decode resolution imposes -- roughly 1.62 composite of headroom remains. Raising the ceiling to 128x256 (oracle composite 0.1638) buys nothing the model can currently use.
ideas.scope_of_negative_evidence: Refutes decoder output resolution as the CURRENT binding constraint, at the present error level, for this target and metric. It does NOT say a finer decode is worthless forever: if a future model approaches d1 ~ 0.9, the 64x128 ceiling becomes real and this idea should be revived. Recorded as a ceiling to re-check, not a closed door.
ideas.next_action: none. Revive only if achieved d1 approaches ~0.9.

ideas.origin: unexplained result from E0/E1 (best at epoch 14 of 26, then 12 epochs of overfitting)
ideas.target_bottleneck: the 1h wall-clock budget is spent on epochs that make the model worse
ideas.general_hypothesis: A schedule sized for a different epoch count is not a schedule. The cosine LR is written for --epochs 40 but only ~26 fit the wall-clock budget, so LR never anneals and training ends mid-schedule at a degraded point, while the last ~12 epochs actively overfit.
ideas.detailed_hypothesis: Set --epochs to what actually fits (~24-26) so cosine anneals to zero inside the budget. The annealed tail is where most models make their final, best-generalising progress; recovering it should improve the composite at zero extra compute.
ideas.expected_metric_effect: composite improves; the best epoch moves to near the LAST epoch instead of epoch 14. If the best epoch stays early, the cause is overfitting, not the schedule, and weight decay / EMA is the right lever instead.
ideas.main_falsification_condition: with an annealed schedule the best epoch is still ~14/26 -- the model overfits regardless of LR, so the schedule was never the problem.
ideas.minimal_falsifiable_test: one run of the E1 config with --epochs 26. Compare best_epoch and composite.
ideas.evidence_required: best_epoch moves late AND composite improves beyond sigma=0.008.
ideas.next_action: queue after the RayDPT planar re-anchor (E4); this is a confound affecting EVERY future run, so fix it early

ideas.origin: own reasoning, refuted before consuming any GPU time
ideas.target_bottleneck: (claimed) the net wastes capacity learning cube-edge creases
ideas.general_hypothesis: planar = radial * linf with linf = max(|dx|,|dy|,|dz|) a fixed per-pixel field; predict the smooth radial field and multiply by the known geometry so the creases come for free.
ideas.why_dropped: The premise is measurably false. Grad-energy over 24 samples / 6 scenes: planar 0.01618, radial 0.01677; radial is smoother in only 17% of samples (mean ratio 1.035). PLANAR is already the smoother parameterisation -- for an axis-aligned room a wall perpendicular to a cube face has CONSTANT planar depth, while radial curves as 1/cos. Reparameterising to radial would make the regression HARDER.
ideas.scope_of_negative_evidence: Refutes THIS reparameterisation under the planar target on SoundSpaces rooms (mostly axis-aligned). It says nothing about geometry-conditioned heads in general, and nothing about scenes with non-axis-aligned walls. Do not generalise it into 'geometric conditioning does not help'.
ideas.next_action: none. Revive only with new evidence (e.g. a non-axis-aligned benchmark).

ideas.origin: reading the code against the qualitative figure (predictions show quasi-periodic azimuthal banding)
ideas.target_bottleneck: RayDPT's DPT skip connections impose a FALSE spatial correspondence between the spectrogram's axes and the ERP's axes
ideas.general_hypothesis: A skip connection asserts that a pixel at (i,j) in the encoder feature means the same thing as the pixel at (i,j) in the output. That assertion is only valid when input and output share a coordinate system. Here they do not: the input's height is FREQUENCY and its width is TIME, while the output's height is ELEVATION and its width is AZIMUTH. Adding one to the other by pixel index is a category error, and it may be actively injecting structure that has no geometric meaning.
ideas.detailed_hypothesis: _features() produces (C, F=257, T=18) and interpolates it to (C,256,512): height <- frequency, width <- time (nearest-upsampled 28.4x, so the width axis is a staircase of 18 constant blocks). RayDPT then does `m16 = F16 + se4(e4)` and `up(m16) + F32 + se3(e3)` -- adding encoder features to the ray-query grid at matching pixel coordinates. This ties azimuth to echo arrival TIME and elevation to FREQUENCY. The ray-conditioned cross-attention (kv_e4/kv_e3) does NOT have this problem: it is global, so it only sees an unordered token set and can learn any mapping. The DPT skips are therefore the one place where a false geometry is hard-coded. Predicted signature: the predicted depth maps should show azimuthal banding with a period tied to the 18-block staircase (~28.4 px at full width, ~7.1 px at e2's 128-wide grid), which is visible in out/display/qualitative.png.
ideas.expected_metric_effect: Removing or re-routing the spatial skips should reduce azimuthal banding. d1 (angular assignment) should improve; RMSE may be unchanged. If banding persists without the skips, the false correspondence was not its cause.
ideas.main_falsification_condition: An FFT of the predicted depth along azimuth shows NO peak at the 18-block period, i.e. the banding is not tied to the staircase -- then the story is wrong even if ablating skips helps for other reasons.
ideas.minimal_falsifiable_test: DIAGNOSTIC FIRST, zero GPU: take the existing batvision predictions and FFT them along the azimuth axis; check for a spectral peak at period ~28.4 px (or its harmonics) that is absent in the GT's azimuthal spectrum. Only if the peak exists is the architecture change worth an hour. NOTE the diagnostic works on batvision too -- it also consumes the same (freq,time) image -- so it tests the REPRESENTATION's staircase, not RayDPT's skips. To isolate the skips, a second arm ablating se2/se3/se4 on RayDPT is needed.
ideas.budget_risk: low for the diagnostic (no GPU). Medium for the ablation: removing skips changes RayDPT's parameter count and may need its own re-tune.
ideas.evidence_required: an azimuthal spectral peak at the staircase period in predictions but not in GT; then a RayDPT skip-ablation that reduces it.
ideas.next_action: none. Do not spend GPU on the skip ablation on this rationale. Revive only with an independent reason.
ideas.diagnostic_result: Pre-registered signature ABSENT. Azimuthal FFT of E3 batvision predictions (24 val samples): at the staircase frequency k=18 (period 28.4 px) the local prominence is 0.806 in PRED (a dip, not a peak) vs 1.041 in GT; pred/gt power ratio at k=18 is 0.05, the same as its neighbours k=17,19. No harmonic peaks at k=36/54 either. The nearest-upsampled 18-block staircase is NOT reproduced in the output.
ideas.scope_of_negative_evidence: Refutes the predicted SIGNATURE (staircase banding), measured on batvision -- which also mixes encoder features into the decoder by pixel index via U-Net skips, so the absence of the peak is real evidence against 'pixel-index skips inject the staircase'. It does NOT test RayDPT's DPT skips directly, and it does not refute the underlying axis-mismatch concern, which now has NO supporting evidence and no cheap test. Per the pre-registered rule, the story as stated is wrong even if a skip ablation later helps for other reasons. Parked, not pursued.

ideas.origin: metric decomposition + azimuthal spectrum of predictions (zero GPU)
ideas.target_bottleneck: the objective devotes most of its gradient to low-frequency terms, so the model may be trained to be blurry
ideas.general_hypothesis: A model fits what the loss measures. If most of the training signal comes from deliberately low-frequency views of the prediction, the model has little incentive to produce high-frequency structure, and will converge to a low-pass answer even when the target is sharp.
ideas.detailed_hypothesis: composite_loss = 1.0*dense_MAE + 1.0*coarse_layout(16x32) + 0.5*low_pass(sigma=3). MEASURED at E3's convergence (mae 0.0392, lc 0.0360, llow 0.0370): the two low-frequency auxiliaries contribute 58.2% of the total loss (coarse 38.4%, low-pass 19.7%), leaving only 41.8% to the full-resolution dense term. program.md states lc/llow are auxiliary regularisers, free to tune or zero. Zeroing them should let the dense term dominate and restore high-frequency azimuthal content.
ideas.expected_metric_effect: Predicted azimuthal power above k=6 rises toward GT's; d1 improves (blur is what a +-25% threshold metric punishes). If d1 falls, the auxiliaries were load-bearing regularisers, not a bias.
ideas.main_falsification_condition: With w_coarse_layout=0 and w_low=0 the predicted azimuthal spectrum still collapses to k<=6. Then the objective is NOT the cause of the low-pass behaviour, and I7 (binaural angular under-determination) is the surviving explanation.
ideas.minimal_falsifiable_test: one run of run_base.py (cheapest parent, E3 config) with --w-coarse-layout 0 --w-low 0; recompute the azimuthal spectrum with the same diagnostic. Requires exposing the two loss weights as CLI flags (they are currently hard-coded in make_config).
ideas.evidence_required: a measurable rise in predicted azimuthal power for k>6, together with a d1 that does not regress.
ideas.next_action: expose w_coarse_layout / w_low as CLI flags, then queue the ablation after the I1 arms.

ideas.origin: physics of the sensor, prompted by the azimuthal spectrum
ideas.target_bottleneck: two microphones may fundamentally under-determine high azimuthal frequencies
ideas.general_hypothesis: Azimuth is recovered only from interaural differences between TWO receivers. A two-element array has a broad, smooth directional response, so fine angular structure in the scene maps onto almost identical binaural cues. If so, high azimuthal frequencies are not merely hard to learn -- they are close to unobservable, and no loss or decoder can conjure them.
ideas.detailed_hypothesis: MEASURED: the predicted azimuthal power spectrum holds 97.4% of its non-DC power in k<=6 while GT holds 75.8%; the model over-represents k=4 (pred/gt 1.94), which corresponds to the four walls of a room -- genuine coarse geometry -- and under-represents everything finer (pred/gt ~0.05 for k>=17). The cutoff k~6 corresponds to ~60 degrees of azimuth, a plausible effective beamwidth for a two-element array.
ideas.expected_metric_effect: If true, d1 is capped by angular observability, and the productive levers are the REPRESENTATION (cues that carry more angular information) rather than the decoder or the loss.
ideas.main_falsification_condition: I6's ablation restores high-frequency azimuthal content. Then the low-pass behaviour was an artefact of the objective, not of the sensor, and I7 is dropped.
ideas.minimal_falsifiable_test: I6's ablation is the discriminating experiment for BOTH ideas: it cleanly separates 'the loss made it blurry' from 'the sensor cannot see it'. Run I6 first; I7 needs no separate run to be tested.
ideas.evidence_required: survives if and only if I6's ablation fails to restore high-frequency content.
ideas.next_action: none directly -- I7 is decided by I6's outcome. Do NOT call this a task ceiling; it is a scoped hypothesis about angular observability with two receivers.

ideas.origin: E4, the first RayDPT run under the planar target
ideas.target_bottleneck: RayDPT is COMPUTE-STARVED under the fixed 1-hour wall-clock budget
ideas.general_hypothesis: When the budget is wall-clock, throughput IS accuracy. A model that fits 5 epochs cannot be compared with one that fits 25; the slower model is penalised for its speed, not judged on its mechanism. Before any RayDPT mechanism can be evaluated, RayDPT must be able to converge inside the budget.
ideas.detailed_hypothesis: E4 ran 713.5 s/epoch vs batvision's ~130 s, so only 5 epochs fit against batvision's 25. Its best checkpoint is the LAST epoch and every val metric was still improving monotonically (rmse 1.485->1.424->1.399, d1 0.513->0.530->0.542). So E4's composite 2.0471 vs E3's 1.8567 measures budget, not architecture. MEASURED module cost (CPU forward, indicative): the 64x128 scale dominates -- lsa64 29.3% + cross64 27.7% = 57% of forward. LocalSphericalAttention builds its neighbourhood with F.unfold, materialising (B, C*win*win, H*W): 1.8 GB at 64x128 for k and v at batch 16, which also explains the 16.2 GB peak VRAM. Levers, in order of expected value/risk: bf16 autocast; replace the unfold in _window_kv with a gather/shift-based neighbourhood; drop or cheapen the 64-scale cross-attention.
ideas.expected_metric_effect: epochs_ran rises toward batvision's; the composite improves BECAUSE the model reaches convergence, not because the mechanism changed. Report epochs_ran alongside every number.
ideas.main_falsification_condition: Throughput improves but the composite does not, AND the best epoch is again the last -- then RayDPT is not merely starved and its per-epoch learning is genuinely weaker.
ideas.minimal_falsifiable_test: bf16 autocast alone (smallest diff, no architecture change), measuring s/epoch, epochs_ran and the composite. A pure speed change that leaves the mechanism identical is the cleanest test of the starvation hypothesis.
ideas.budget_risk: none -- this idea IS about the budget.
ideas.evidence_required: epochs_ran increases materially AND the best epoch stops being the last one (i.e. the run actually converges).
ideas.next_action: HIGHEST VALUE. Queue after the I1 arms: bf16 autocast on train.py, then re-measure.

ideas.general_hypothesis: program.md: a gain from deleting code is the best kind. Dead parameters are not free -- they occupy VRAM, they mislead every parameter-count comparison, and they hide the model's real size from the researcher.
ideas.detailed_hypothesis: UNet8Encoder builds e1..e8 but RayDPT.forward() only consumes e1..e4. VERIFIED: e5..e8 hold 16.781M of the model's 24.439M parameters (68.7%) and receive NO gradient after a backward pass (p.grad is None). They cost ~67 MB of VRAM and no compute. Deleting them is provably output-equivalent.
ideas.expected_metric_effect: EXACTLY ZERO change to any metric -- that is the point, and it is the test. Model size drops 24.44M -> 7.66M; VRAM falls slightly.
ideas.main_falsification_condition: Any metric changes at all. That would mean e5..e8 were reachable after all, and the deletion is wrong.
ideas.minimal_falsifiable_test: Delete e5..e8; assert the forward output is bit-identical on a fixed input with a fixed seed. No training run needed to verify correctness -- only to confirm the score is unchanged.
ideas.next_action: Fold into the I8 throughput study as a free simplification; verify bit-identical output before training.

discrepancies.observation: log1p magnitude compression is a no-op at 2ch: E1 (log) composite 1.8784 vs E0 (nolog) 1.8854, delta 0.0070 < sigma 0.008. The two runs SPLIT the underlying metrics -- log wins rmse (1.3116 vs 1.3186) and d1 (0.5808 vs 0.5785), nolog wins abs_rel (0.4143 vs 0.4211).
discrepancies.why_it_matters: The pre-registered detailed hypothesis predicted log would help MORE at 2ch than at 5ch, because in the 5ch stack ILD already supplies a log-domain ratio. At 2ch it does not help at all, so that half of the prediction is unsupported. A metric split with a sub-sigma delta is the signature of noise, not of a mechanism.
discrepancies.diagnostic_value: If E2/E3 show log ALSO does nothing at 5ch, then dynamic-range compression is simply irrelevant to this task -- which would point at time-of-flight (I1), not level statistics, as the operative cue. That is a cheap, strong inference from data already being collected.
discrepancies.next_action: none. A third draw would crown the log effect as an independent finding; low priority since it is already used in the reference champion.
discrepancies.resolution: Closed by E3. log1p helps by +0.0070 (2ch) and +0.0078 (5ch): each draw at/below sigma~0.008, but consistent in SIGN, MAGNITUDE and METRIC DIRECTION across two independent channel counts, and the metric it moves is RMSE (-0.0070/-0.0119) -- i.e. RANGE, exactly what the dynamic-range mechanism predicts. So log is a real small effect, NOT noise. What IS refuted is the predicted interaction: log did not help more at 2ch than at 5ch (0.0070 vs 0.0078), so ILD's log-domain ratio does not make magnitude compression redundant. The effects are additive.

discrepancies.observation: E2 (5ch nolog, composite 1.8646) beats both 2ch cells (1.8854, 1.8784) by 0.014-0.021 -- above the sigma~0.008 floor -- but the win comes ENTIRELY from d1 (0.5938 vs 0.5808/0.5785). Its RMSE is actually WORSE than E1's (1.3207 vs 1.3116) and its ABS_REL is the worst of the three (0.4460 vs 0.4143/0.4211).
discrepancies.why_it_matters: The composite weights d1 at 1/0.46, so a d1 gain of 0.013 alone buys 0.028 of composite -- enough to swamp the RMSE regression. So 'derived interaural cues help' is true ONLY in the d1 sense: the cues make more pixels land within +-25% of truth, while the typical squared error gets slightly larger. That is the profile of a mechanism that improves DIRECTIONAL/angular assignment (which is exactly what ILD and IPD encode: azimuth) without improving absolute RANGE. It is not evidence that the cues help distance estimation.
discrepancies.diagnostic_value: Sharpens I1. If the interaural cues fix azimuth but leave range untouched, then range accuracy is limited by something else -- and time-of-flight resolution (I1) is the physically obvious candidate. It also warns that a composite win can be a single-metric win: never report 'E2 is better' without saying better AT WHAT.
discrepancies.next_action: This is now the decision rule for I1: a temporal-resolution gain MUST appear in RMSE (range), not d1. If it appears in d1 it is not acting through time-of-flight. And since log already buys range by making faint far echoes readable, I1's window-smear mechanism is a DIFFERENT range constraint -- if both hold, a combine study is justified.
discrepancies.resolution: Confirmed and generalised by the full 2x2. The dissociation is clean and reproduces at both log settings: interaural cues move d1 (+0.0153/+0.0141, angle) with rmse flat (+0.0021/-0.0028); log1p moves rmse (-0.0070/-0.0119, range) with d1 flat (+0.0023/+0.0011). Cues fix AZIMUTH, compression fixes RANGE. They fix different failure modes, which is why the effects are additive rather than redundant.

discrepancies.observation: Both 2ch cells peak at epoch 14 of 26 and both peak at exactly 2400.3 MB VRAM.
discrepancies.why_it_matters: The overfitting turn and the memory envelope are properties of the architecture + schedule, NOT of the input representation. This makes epoch count a CONFOUND for every comparison run under the fixed wall-clock budget: any change that slows an epoch silently reduces the epochs that fit, and is penalised for reasons unrelated to its mechanism.
discrepancies.diagnostic_value: Any future structural change that costs wall-clock (e.g. I2's extra attention scale) must report epochs-fit alongside the composite, or its result is uninterpretable.
discrepancies.next_action: fix via I3 (match --epochs to the budget); until then, always log best_epoch and epochs_ran

discrepancies.observation: Predicted depth maps in out/display/qualitative.png are low-frequency blobs showing quasi-periodic banding along azimuth, while the GT is sharp and piecewise-constant. The 5ch predictions carry visibly more azimuthal structure than the 2ch ones.
discrepancies.why_it_matters: The input's width axis is STFT TIME with only 18 distinct frames, nearest-upsampled 28.4x into 512 columns -- a staircase. If the predicted banding period matches that staircase, the model is reproducing an artefact of the interpolation rather than scene geometry, and the nearest-neighbour upsample in _features() (editable research logic) is itself a defect. The extra 5ch structure is consistent with S0's finding that interaural cues buy angular assignment.
discrepancies.diagnostic_value: Distinguishes two very different stories: (a) the model is under-fitting and simply produces blurry output; (b) the representation injects a spatial artefact the model faithfully reproduces. These call for opposite interventions.
discrepancies.next_action: none. Superseded by I6 vs I7.
discrepancies.resolution: The banding is NOT an interpolation artefact. Azimuthal FFT (24 val samples, E3 batvision): no peak at the 18-block staircase frequency k=18 (prominence 0.806, i.e. a dip) nor at its harmonics. What I read as banding is the k=4 component -- the four walls of a room -- which the model OVER-represents (pred/gt 1.94). The real characterisation: the model is a LOW-PASS predictor, holding 97.4% of its azimuthal power in k<=6 versus GT's 75.8%, and only ~5% of GT's power at k>=17. This resolves D4 and spawns two competing explanations, I6 (the objective is 58% low-frequency) and I7 (two microphones under-determine fine azimuth), discriminated by I6's ablation.

discrepancies.observation: E4 (RayDPT) fitted only 5 epochs in the 1-hour budget (713.5 s/epoch) versus batvision's 25 (~130 s/epoch). Its best checkpoint is the LAST epoch and all val metrics were still improving monotonically. Peak VRAM 16.2 GB vs 2.4 GB.
discrepancies.why_it_matters: E4's composite 2.0471 vs E3's 1.8567 CANNOT be read as 'RayDPT is the worse model'. One model converged and then overfit for 12 epochs; the other never reached convergence. Under a wall-clock budget, throughput is silently part of the score. Every RayDPT-vs-batvision statement in this phase must carry epochs_ran, and no RayDPT mechanism can be fairly judged until RayDPT can converge inside the budget.
discrepancies.diagnostic_value: Reframes the research: the first-order problem for RayDPT is not its ray conditioning, its decoder, or its representation -- it is throughput (I8). It also retro-explains why D2 (batvision's overfitting turn at epoch 13-14) and this are the same confound seen from opposite ends: batvision has budget to spare, RayDPT has none.
discrepancies.next_action: I8: bf16 autocast as the minimal pure-speed change; re-measure s/epoch, epochs_ran, best_epoch. Also I9: delete the 16.78M dead encoder params.
